1	# Artifact Generalization & Three-Layer Run Architecture
2	
3	Date: 2026-04-29
4	Status: Approved (refined through 3 review rounds with codex + fresh-eyes)
5	
6	## Summary
7	
8	Two threads converge in this plan:
9	
10	1. **Generalize artifacts beyond markdown documents.** Diagrams, wireframes, hero images, prompts — text and binary — become first-class artifacts via media-type plurality and sidecar `.ddx.yaml` files. Generated artifacts are checked into git; regeneration is source-hash-driven.
11	2. **Three-layer run architecture.** DDx owns three explicit layered primitives: `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx try <bead>` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). The current two-class storage split (`.ddx/exec-runs/` vs `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/`) collapses into one substrate.
12	
13	These threads converge: **generating an artifact is a layer-1 agent run** with `produces_artifact` metadata.
14	
15	## Decisions (resolved through review)
16	
17	- **Artifact authority:** identity present → artifact. No "managed asset" second-class concept.
18	- **FEAT-019 fate:** child of FEAT-010 (not peer). Contracts to evaluation UX; workflow shapes (comparison, replay, benchmark) move to skills library.
19	- **Server posture:** 100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP for all DDx state, sequenced by impact. Writes case-by-case as workflows demand. Only `artifactRegenerate` write surface added in this plan.
20	- **Generated artifacts:** committed to git unconditionally. Size guard (50MB warn / 100MB hard error) with actionable git-lfs guidance; no auto-init / auto-`.gitattributes`.
21	- **CONTRACT-003:** amendments allowed if FEAT-006 work surfaces gaps. The mountable Cobra root export (below) is a load-bearing audit item.
22	- **Run substrate:** single on-disk shape; layer-2 and layer-3 add metadata. `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` collapse.
23	- **No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.** Comparison, replay, benchmark, etc. are skill compositions, never enshrined in Go core or specs.
24	- **Loop ownership sharpened:** DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (e.g., "comparison failed → enqueue reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory.
25	- **CLI verbs:** `ddx run` / `ddx try` / `ddx work` promoted to top-level. Hard-deprecate `ddx agent run`, `ddx agent execute-bead`, `ddx agent execute-loop` (exit non-zero with bare redirect message — no aliases, no grace period).
26	- **Generate-artifact layer placement:** pure-return generators (agent returns bytes/text, DDx writes file) are layer-1. Repo-editing generators are layer-2 with bead-less worktree.
27	- **Quorum dispatch:** moves to skill composition. `--quorum` flag goes away; replaced by `compare-prompts` skill.
28	- **`ddx agent` passthrough is structural:** DDx imports the upstream agent module's Cobra root and mounts it under `ddx agent`. Notionally `cli(ddx).agent(load_cli(agent))`. DDx does not enumerate per-subcommand wrappers; the entire upstream tree is the source of truth.
29	
30	## Three-layer architecture
31	
32	| Layer | CLI | Inputs | Outputs / side effects | Owns |
33	|---|---|---|---|---|
34	| 1 | `ddx run` | prompt + agent config | structured output, side effects, run metadata (tokens, model, duration, exit) | invocation atom; consumes upstream `ddx-agent` per CONTRACT-003 |
35	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | bead id, base revision | new worktree state + agent-run evidence; merge or preserve | worktree start/end capture; bead → prompt resolution; side-effect bundling |
36	| 3 | `ddx work` | bead queue, stop conditions | sequence of `ddx try` records + loop-level record (drained / blocked / deferred) | mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection |
37	
38	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records.
39	
40	`ddx artifact regenerate <id>` is sugar over layer 1 (or layer 2 if the generator edits the repo) with `produces_artifact: <id>` metadata.
41	
42	## Frame artifact updates
43	
44	### docs/helix/00-discover/product-vision.md
45	- Thesis: "documents AI agents consume" → "artifacts agents produce and consume."
46	- Artifact-management bullet: non-document media + generators.
47	- One sentence on producer/consumer space (no separate matrix).
48	- New Design-Philosophy subsection: **Three-layer run architecture** — `run` / `try` / `work` named as DDx's loop ownership.
49	- KVPs: "Multi-media artifact graph"; "Three-layer run architecture (run/try/work)"; note invocation is upstream.
50	- Principle #1: "Artifacts are the product (documents primary)."
51	
52	### docs/helix/01-frame/prd.md
53	- Summary: artifacts (multi-media); three-layer run architecture; one substrate.
54	- Problem: add "no provenance for generated artifacts."
55	- Goals: reword #1; add three-layer-architecture goal; add generated-artifact-regeneration goal; add "100% read coverage on HTTP/MCP."
56	- Non-goals: sharpen loop wording (mechanical drain in scope; supervisory content-aware decisions out). Add: "DDx does not catalog run types beyond the three layers."
57	- FEAT-001/005/006/007/008/010/019/021 blurbs updated.
58	
59	### FEAT-001-cli.md
60	- Promote `run` / `try` / `work` to top-level.
61	- `ddx agent` mounts upstream Cobra root structurally; no DDx-defined leaf subcommands beneath it.
62	- Hard-deprecation handlers for `ddx agent {run, execute-bead, execute-loop}`.
63	- New namespaces: `ddx runs` (cross-layer evidence introspection), `ddx tries` (layer-2 specifically), `ddx work workers` (layer-3 worker management).
64	- Remove FEAT-001:92 backward-compatibility clause for `ddx agent execute-loop`.
65	
66	### FEAT-005-artifacts.md
67	- Identity broadens to non-markdown via sidecar `.ddx.yaml`.
68	- Add `media_type` and `generated_by` fields.
69	- Authority rule documented: identity present → artifact.
70	- Terminology cleanup ("identity," not "ddx: identity").
71	
72	### FEAT-006-agent-service.md
73	- Tighten to layer-1 consumer-side wrapper that powers `ddx run`.
74	- `ddx agent` is structural passthrough (mount upstream Cobra root).
75	- Non-bead Profile/Permissions selection (artifact-keyed path).
76	- Migration table: what stays in FEAT-006 (CONTRACT-003 boundary, profile/permissions), what moves to FEAT-010 (worktree, merge/preserve, evidence bundles, queue drain), what moves to FEAT-001 (CLI surface).
77	- Session-log boundary clarified: DDx owns the envelope/pointer in evidence bundles; upstream owns inner log shape.
78	
79	### FEAT-010-executions.md (substantial refactor)
80	- Three-layer architecture explicit and load-bearing.
81	- Substrate unification: one record shape; layer metadata.
82	- Migrate `.ddx/exec-runs/` and `.ddx/executions/<attempt-id>/` to one layout.
83	- Specify `ddx work` no-progress / stop conditions.
84	- Narrow read-only amendment for `artifactRegenerate` only.
85	- No run-type catalog beyond the three layers.
86	
87	### FEAT-007-doc-graph.md
88	- Sidecar scanner refactor; `media_type`; `generated_by` edge with separate staleness rule (provenance, not authority — does not cascade like `depends_on`); version bump on Complete-status feature.
89	- 100% read endpoints for graph state, generated-artifact metadata, sidecar content.
90	
91	### FEAT-008-web-ui.md
92	- Media-type-aware rendering (markdown, mermaid SVG, excalidraw embed, image inline, PDF embed).
93	- Regenerate action wired to `artifactRegenerate` mutation; reconcile with existing FEAT-008:391 weak Re-run requirement.
94	- Layer-aware run views (`work` → `try` → `run` drill-down).
95	
96	### FEAT-019-agent-evaluation.md
97	- Contract to evaluation UX (comparison views, grading rubric storage and display, benchmark result aggregation).
98	- Workflow shapes → skills library.
99	- Remove peer-to-FEAT-010 assertions (FEAT-019:40, :404).
100	
101	### FEAT-021-dashboard-ui.md
102	- Layer-aware run-history routes against unified substrate.
103	
104	### FEAT-014, FEAT-013
105	- One-sentence touches: cost attribution and concurrency for generate-artifact.
106	
107	## `ddx agent` subcommand fates
108	
109	`ddx agent` becomes a structural mount of the upstream agent CLI Cobra root. Per-file fate in DDx:
110	
111	**Delete with prejudice (upstream owns these via mounted subtree):**
112	- `agent_list.go`
113	- `agent_capabilities.go`
114	- `agent_doctor.go`
115	- `agent_check.go`
116	- `agent_models.go`
117	- `agent_providers.go`
118	- `agent_route_status.go`
119	- `agent_catalog.go`
120	- `agent_reindex.go`
121	- `agent_benchmark.go` (replaced by `benchmark-suite` skill)
122	- `agent_replay.go` (replaced by `replay-bead` skill)
123	
124	**Move to other DDx namespaces (DDx-only concerns):**
125	- `agent_executions.go` → `tries_*.go`
126	- `agent_log.go` → `runs_log.go`
127	- `agent_metrics.go` → `runs_metrics.go`
128	- `agent_usage.go` → folded into `runs_metrics.go`
129	- `agent_workers.go` → `work_workers.go`
130	
131	**DDx-side overrides on the mounted subtree:**
132	- Deprecation handlers for `agent run` / `agent execute-bead` / `agent execute-loop`.
133	- `agent condense` (DDx-specific output filter — keep as override or move upstream during impl pass).
134	
135	**`agent_cmd.go` itself thins to:** mount upstream Cobra root + register DDx overrides.
136	
137	## Skill library (DDx-shipped workflows)
138	
139	Per FEAT-011's path model: source at `skills/ddx/`, embedded at `cli/internal/skills/ddx/`, init-time copies to `.claude/skills/ddx/`, `.agents/skills/ddx/`, `.ddx/skills/ddx/`.
140	
141	- `compare-prompts` — N-arm dispatch + aggregation (replaces `--quorum` flag and `agent benchmark`)
142	- `replay-bead` — re-run with altered conditions, baseline diff (replaces `agent replay`)
143	- `benchmark-suite` — compare across prompt matrix
144	- `adversarial-review`
145	- `effort-estimate`
146	- `bead-breakdown`
147	
148	## Bead breakdown
149	
150	**Frame updates (sequential where dependencies require):**
151	
152	1. Update product-vision.md (+ three-layer subsection)
153	2. Update prd.md (+ three-layer goal, sharpened loop non-goal, no-catalog non-goal)
154	3. Update FEAT-001 (CLI surface refactor; remove backcompat clause; new namespaces) — **hard predecessor for #6, #7**
155	4. Update FEAT-005 (multi-media identity refactor)
156	5. CONTRACT-003 audit — confirm `ddx-agent` exports a mountable Cobra root and other gaps
157	6. *(Conditional)* Open CONTRACT-003 amendment in `~/Projects/agent` if #5 surfaces gaps
158	7. Update FEAT-006 (layer-1 consumer; structural passthrough; non-bead Profile selection; migration table; session-log envelope boundary)
159	8. Refactor FEAT-010 (three-layer architecture; substrate unification; on-disk migration; no-progress stop conditions; narrow `artifactRegenerate` amendment)
160	9. Update FEAT-007 (sidecar refactor; media_type; generated_by; separate staleness; version bump)
161	10. Contract FEAT-019 to evaluation UX
162	11. Update FEAT-008 (media rendering, regenerate action, layer-aware run views)
163	12. Update FEAT-021 (layer-aware run-history routes)
164	13. Update FEAT-014 (cost attribution for generate-artifact)
165	14. Update FEAT-013 (concurrency note for regenerate)
166	15. **Repo-wide audit & verb migration** — atomic with #3:
167	    - Delete DDx reimplementations of upstream subcommands (list above).
168	    - Migrate DDx-only run/evidence subcommands to `runs`/`tries`/`work workers`.
169	    - Register DDx overrides on the mounted subtree (deprecations + condense).
170	    - Delete `agent_reindex.go` outright.
171	    - Update scripts/hooks/library skills/personas/docs/README/demos to new verbs.
172	
173	**Design artifacts (concurrent with frame):**
174	
175	16. Architecture: artifact identity schema (concurrent with #4)
176	17. Architecture: graph schema with media_type + generated_by + separate staleness (concurrent with #9)
177	18. Architecture: three-layer run substrate — record shape, layer metadata, on-disk layout, migration, `ddx work` stop conditions (concurrent with #8)
178	19. Sidecar `.ddx.yaml` schema design
179	
180	**Read-coverage thread:**
181	
182	20. Read-coverage audit — enumerate every CLI-visible read; map to HTTP/MCP coverage; identify gaps
183	21. Per-gap beads — sequenced by user-visibility impact, land case-by-case (this is a pointer to ongoing work, not a single bead)
184	
185	**Skill library:**
186	
187	22. `compare-prompts` skill
188	23. `replay-bead` skill
189	24. `benchmark-suite` skill
190	25. `adversarial-review` skill
191	26. `effort-estimate` skill
192	27. `bead-breakdown` skill
193	
194	**Implementation (P2, after frame & design):**
195	
196	- CLI: `ddx run` / `ddx try` top-level commands; mount upstream agent Cobra root; deprecation handlers
197	- CLI: `ddx runs` / `ddx tries` / `ddx work workers` namespaces (subsuming migrated subcommands)
198	- CLI: `ddx artifact regenerate <id>`, `ddx artifact list --media-type <t>`
199	- Storage: sidecar reader/writer; size-guard threshold/warning/tests
200	- Graph: `generated_by` edges + provenance staleness rule
201	- Run framework: refactor to one substrate per #18; generator metadata; `ddx work` no-progress detection
202	- Server: `artifactRegenerate` write endpoint
203	- Server UI: media-type renderers; regenerate action; layer-aware run history
204	- Migration: passive `media_type` only (read-time inference from extension); on-disk run-record migration per #18
205	
206	## Constraints
207	
208	- Beads #3, #7, #8, #15 must merge atomically — old verbs are referenced in scripts/library/personas/init help text/CI; an isolated #3 break the in-flight `ddx-refine-plan` skill that produced this plan.
209	- Bead #5 (CONTRACT-003 audit) gates #7 (FEAT-006). #6 conditional.
210	- Architecture sketches (#16–#19) run concurrent with their corresponding frame beads, not after — keeps frame/design from drifting.
211	- Read-coverage epic (#20–#21) is independent and not gated on this plan.
212	
213	## Refinement provenance
214	
215	Refined through 3 rounds of adversarial review (codex via `ddx agent run --harness codex` + fresh-eyes general-purpose subagent in parallel) plus user-resolved questions Q1–Q12. Key user decisions: artifact authority (Q1), FEAT-019 child-of-010 (Q2), reads-matter-writes-case-by-case (Q3), check-in unconditionally (Q4), CONTRACT-003 amendments allowed (Q5), substrate unification (Q6), no run-type catalog (Q7), no v1-gate framing (Q8), FEAT-019 contracts (Q9), generate-artifact split layer-1/layer-2 (Q10), quorum-as-skill (Q11), structural passthrough not per-command (Q12 follow-up).

6	---
7	# Product Requirements Document: DDx
8	
9	**Version:** 4.1.0
10	**Date:** 2026-04-06
11	**Status:** Active
12	
13	## Summary
14	
15	DDx is a monorepo producing three artifacts that together form the shared
16	local-first infrastructure for document-driven development:
17	
18	1. **`ddx` CLI** — document library management, artifact graph operations, bead
19	   tracking, agent dispatch, execution definitions and runs, persona
20	   composition, template application, and git sync
21	2. **`ddx-server`** — web server + MCP endpoints for browsing documents,
22	   artifacts, beads, agent session logs, and execution history over the network
23	3. **`ddx.github.io`** — promotional website explaining DDx to developers and
24	   linking to docs
25	
26	DDx is the foundation layer. Workflow tools (HELIX, others) build on top. DDx
27	provides reusable local services; it does not impose workflow phases or
28	methodology.
29	
30	Concrete command, API, and storage contracts belong in the DDx feature
31	specifications. The PRD stays at the user- and capability-level:
32	
33	- FEAT-001 defines the CLI surface and operator experience
34	- FEAT-002 defines the server, HTTP, and MCP surfaces
35	- FEAT-003 defines the promotional website and documentation
36	- FEAT-004 defines shared work-item storage
37	- FEAT-005 defines the artifact convention and frontmatter schema
38	- FEAT-006 defines agent dispatch and session evidence
39	- FEAT-007 defines the artifact graph and staleness model
40	- FEAT-008 defines the embedded web UI for browsing and managing project state
41	- FEAT-009 defines the online library and plugin registry
42	- FEAT-010 defines generic execution definitions and immutable run history
43	- FEAT-011 defines agent-facing skills for DDx CLI operations
44	- FEAT-012 defines git awareness: auto-commit for documents and bead tracker,
45	  document history, write-then-commit for MCP/UI clients, and agent guidance
46	  generation on init
47	- FEAT-013 defines multi-agent coordination: concurrent bead safety,
48	  MCP supervisor surface, worktree-aware dispatch
49	- FEAT-014 defines agent token awareness: usage tracking, budget enforcement,
50	  and model selection guidance across harnesses
51	- FEAT-015 defines the installation architecture: clean separation of
52	  install.sh (binary), ddx install --global (skills), ddx init (project),
53	  and ddx install <plugin> (plugin lifecycle)
54	- FEAT-016 defines process metrics: bead lifecycle cost, rework rates, and
55	  derived measures computed from existing stores (beads, agent sessions).
56	  Distinct from FEAT-010 which covers operational metrics you *run*.
57	- ~~FEAT-017~~: adversarial review is a form of multi-agent dispatch covered by
58	  FEAT-006 quorum infrastructure. The "review against governing artifacts →
59	  structured findings → beads" workflow needs a design cycle to find the right
60	  abstraction, not a standalone feature.
61	- FEAT-018 defines plugin API documentation and stability: document existing
62	  extension surfaces (package.yaml, plugin directory layout, SKILL.md, hooks,
63	  bead conventions), add schema versioning, commit to backward compatibility
64	- FEAT-019 defines agent evaluation and prompt comparison: dispatch the same
65	  prompt to multiple harnesses, capture structured outputs, and surface
66	  side-by-side comparisons for human review
67	- FEAT-020 defines server node state and project registry: the server acquires
68	  a stable node identity (hostname or DDX_NODE_NAME), persists a project
69	  registry in an XDG-standard JSON file, writes a discovery addr file, and
70	  CLI commands auto-register their project with the server via a fire-and-
71	  forget background call
72	- FEAT-021 defines the multi-node dashboard UI: extends the FEAT-008 web UI
73	  with node/project-aware routing so every view is bookmarkable
74	  (/nodes/:nodeId/projects/:projectId/...), combined cross-project views for
75	  beads and agent sessions, and project-scoped views for documents, dependency
76	  graph, and commit log
77	
78	## Problem
79	
80	AI-assisted development needs more than prompt files. Teams need a shared way
81	to manage declarative artifacts, reusable runtime evidence, and local
82	automation infrastructure without hardcoding workflow semantics into each tool.
83	
84	- **No structure**: Artifacts, prompts, personas, and patterns accumulate as
85	  ad hoc files with weak identity and no explicit relationships
86	- **No reusable work-item store**: Workflow tools reimplement issue storage,
87	  dependency tracking, and coordination instead of sharing a local substrate
88	- **No reusable agent dispatch**: Each tool grows its own harness registry,
89	  logging, and output-capture behavior
90	- **No reusable execution evidence**: Metrics, checks, and similar operations
91	  fall back to bespoke scripts and logs with no shared history model
92	- **No composition**: Assembling the right combination of persona + pattern +
93	  spec into agent context is manual and error-prone
94	- **No reuse**: Every project reinvents its agent instructions and supporting
95	  mechanics from scratch; proven patterns stay trapped in individual repos
96	- **No network access**: Agents and tools can only read state from the local
97	  filesystem unless projects build their own HTTP or MCP layer
98	- **No discoverability**: Developers can't easily browse what documents,
99	  artifacts, or local runtime evidence are available
100	- **No feedback capture**: Lessons learned from agent interactions, project
101	  completions, and bead lifecycle stay informal — no structured way to capture,
102	  query, or act on what worked and what didn't
103	- **No measurement**: No standard way to track bead lifecycle metrics, token
104	  costs, or plugin-defined measures across projects
105	- **No transferability**: Framework knowledge is trapped in its author;
106	  onboarding new team members requires manual explanation
107	- **No document integrity guarantees**: When an upstream document changes,
108	  dependent documents silently drift — no automatic staleness detection or
109	  reconciliation tasking
110	
111	## Goals
112	
113	### Primary
114	1. **Manage artifacts and document libraries** — provide structure,
115	   conventions, and CLI tooling so declarative project knowledge stays
116	   organized
117	2. **Provide reusable local runtime services** — expose beads, agent dispatch,
118	   and execution history as workflow-agnostic DDx primitives
119	3. **Enable document composition** — combine personas, patterns, specs, and
120	   templates into coherent agent context
121	4. **Serve project state to agents and tools** — expose documents, artifacts,
122	   beads, and execution evidence via MCP endpoints and HTTP
123	5. **Support cross-project reuse** — share document libraries and workflow
124	   plugins through an online registry (`ddx install`)
125	6. **Provide agent-facing skills for DDx operations** — ship interactive
126	   skills (slash commands) that guide agents through complex DDx CLI
127	   operations like bead triage, agent dispatch, and package installation
128	7. **Integrate with revision control** — auto-commit document changes to
129	   protect work, expose document history to agents and tools, enable
130	   write-then-commit workflows for MCP and UI clients
131	8. **Support multi-agent coordination** — make bead operations, document
132	   writes, and agent dispatch safe under concurrent multi-agent use, with
133	   MCP as the remote observation and control surface
134	9. **Embed essential utilities** — bundle common developer tools (jq, etc.)
135	   so workflow tools have a consistent, cross-platform base without external
136	   runtime dependencies
137	10. **Maintain artifact graph integrity** — track relationships between
138	    documents, detect staleness when upstream artifacts change, and generate
139	    reconciliation tasks automatically (FEAT-007)
140	11. **Track process metrics** — derive bead lifecycle cost, rework rates, and
141	    other process measures from existing stores (beads, agent sessions) so
142	    teams can understand the economics and efficiency of their workflow
143	    (FEAT-016)
144	12. **Stabilize the plugin API** — document existing extension surfaces, add
145	    schema versioning, and commit to backward compatibility so plugin authors
146	    can build with confidence (FEAT-018)
147	
148	### Secondary
149	1. **Promote the practice** — website explains document-driven development and
150	   drives adoption
151	2. **Keep artifacts honest** — detect drift between governing documents and
152	   lower-level artifacts or runtime evidence
153	3. **Enable team transferability** — self-documenting project structures,
154	   getting-started guides, and pairing workflows so DDx is productive without
155	   requiring its author in the room
156	
157	### Success Metrics
158	
159	| Metric | Target |
160	|--------|--------|
161	| Time to assemble agent context | <30 seconds |
162	| Document reuse rate across projects | >40% |
163	| MCP endpoint response time | <200ms |
164	| Website explains DDx clearly to new visitor | <2 minutes to understand |
165	
166	### Non-Goals
167	
168	- A workflow methodology (that's HELIX and others, not DDx)
169	- Workflow-specific artifact ladders or stage progression (for example,
170	  `FEAT -> SD -> TD -> TP`) beyond storing IDs and relationships
171	- Workflow-specific bead validation (phase labels, spec-id enforcement — that's
172	  the workflow layer via hooks)
173	- Supervisory loop orchestration — deciding what to do next based on agent or
174	  execution results is workflow-level. DDx provides single-invocation dispatch,
175	  immutable evidence, and mechanical quorum, not decision loops.
176	- An AI agent or agent framework
177	- A standalone desktop GUI for editing documents (the embedded web UI editor
178	  in `ddx-server` is in-scope per FEAT-008; a separate desktop application is not)
179	- A cloud/SaaS service
180	- Real-time collaboration
181	- A storage system — artifacts are versioned in Git; future backends are
182	  possible but not DDx's concern
183	- Defining artifact types or templates — those come from plugins. DDx provides
184	  the infrastructure for storing and relating them.
185	- Operational metric definitions — plugins define what to measure; DDx
186	  provides the execution and collection infrastructure (FEAT-010)
187	- Optimization loop logic — DDx provides primitives (run, measure, compare);
188	  plugins define what to try next and when to converge
189	- Feedback collection features — beads already capture structured feedback;
190	  no separate feedback subsystem needed
191	
192	## Users
193	
194	### Primary: Developer Using AI Agents
195	
196	**Role:** Professional developer directing AI agents and local automation
197	**Goals:** Keep project artifacts organized, compose context quickly, reuse
198	patterns across projects, inspect local execution evidence
199	**Pain:** Documents and evidence scattered everywhere, manual context assembly,
200	reinventing instructions and runtime tooling per project
201	
202	### Secondary: Workflow Tool Author
203	
204	**Role:** Developer building a methodology tool (like HELIX) on DDx primitives
205	**Goals:** Leverage DDx's document management, bead storage, agent dispatch,
206	execution history, persona system, and sync without reimplementing them
207	**Pain:** No standard infrastructure to build on; every workflow tool reinvents
208	local state, execution, and document management
209	
210	### Tertiary: Agent (Machine Consumer)
211	
212	**Role:** AI agent consuming documents via MCP or filesystem
213	**Goals:** Discover available documents and artifacts, read their contents,
214	understand their relationships, and inspect reusable runtime evidence
215	**Pain:** No programmatic way to browse document libraries or local execution
216	history; relies on humans to copy-paste context
217	
218	## Requirements
219	
220	### Must Have (P0)
221	
222	**CLI experience**
223	The exact CLI contract lives in FEAT-001. At the PRD level, DDx must provide a
224	local operator surface that lets users:
225	
226	- initialize and maintain a repo-local DDx workspace
227	- discover, inspect, and manage document-library content and declarative
228	  artifacts
229	- understand artifact relationships, dependency structure, and document
230	  freshness
231	- manage shared work items and their dependencies for higher-level tools
232	- dispatch supported AI agents through one reusable interface and inspect the
233	  resulting evidence
234	- validate installation and configuration health
235	- reuse and update shared DDx library content across projects
236	- invoke DDx operations through agent-facing skills (slash commands) that
237	  provide guided, validated workflows for complex CLI commands
238	- query process metrics (bead lifecycle cost, rework rates) derived from
239	  existing bead and agent session data
240	
241	**Plugin API**
242	The exact API contract lives in FEAT-018. At the PRD level, DDx must provide:
243	
244	- documentation of existing extension surfaces (package.yaml, plugin directory
245	  layout, SKILL.md format, hook scripts, bead conventions)
246	- schema versioning so plugin authors know what they can depend on
247	- backward compatibility commitment for documented surfaces
248	
249	**Server experience**
250	The exact server, HTTP, and MCP contracts live in FEAT-002. At the PRD level,
251	DDx must provide a local network surface that lets tools and agents:
252	
253	- browse and read document-library content remotely
254	- query artifact metadata, relationships, and staleness
255	- inspect shared work-item state
256	- inspect recorded agent session evidence
257	- rely on a stateless, filesystem-backed implementation rather than a hosted
258	  service
259	
260	**Website experience**
261	- Clear explanation of what DDx is and why it exists
262	- Quick start guide
263	- Link to CLI installation
264	- Link to documentation
265	- Embedded terminal recordings (asciinema) demonstrating core workflows
266	- README with animated demos that sell the tool at a glance
267	
268	**Release infrastructure**
269	- CI pipeline that runs the full test suite (via lefthook) on every push and PR
270	- E2E smoke tests validating the install-to-use journey
271	- Automated demo recording regeneration when CLI behavior changes
272	- GitHub Pages deployment gated on CI passing
273	- Multi-platform release builds with changelog generation
274	
275	### Should Have (P1)
276	
277	**CLI experience**
278	The CLI feature spec should also define requirements for:
279	
280	- assembling context from multiple DDx resources
281	- stronger document quality checks and health diagnostics
282	- generic execution definitions and immutable run history for evidence-producing
283	  operations
284	- higher-level projections over execution history for domains such as metrics
285	  and acceptance evidence
286	- process metrics derived from existing stores: bead lifecycle cost (time,
287	  tokens, rework), reopen rates, revision counts (FEAT-016)
288	
289	**Team enablement**
290	- getting-started guides that teach the platform alongside whichever plugin
291	  the user installs
292	- self-documenting project structures — after `ddx init` + plugin install,
293	  the project explains itself to new team members (human or agent)
294	- support for pairing workflows: structured onboarding where an experienced
295	  user guides a new team member through their first project
296	- internal project templates that teams can own end-to-end
297	
298	**Server experience**
299	The server feature spec should also define requirements for:
300	
301	- richer search across document-library contents
302	- persona resolution for remote consumers
303	- read-oriented access to generic execution definitions and run history
304	
305	**Website experience**
306	- Ecosystem page showing workflow tools built on DDx
307	- Document library browser (interactive)
308	- "See It In Action" section with recordings of end-to-end workflows
309	  (init, plugin install, project creation, feature evolution)
310	
311	### Nice to Have (P2)
312	
313	**CLI**
314	- Document testing (validate documents produce expected agent behavior)
315	- Document analytics (most reused, most effective)
316	- IDE integration for document browsing
317	
318	**Server**
319	- WebSocket notifications when documents change
320	- Multi-library aggregation (serve documents from multiple projects)
321	
322	## Constraints
323	
324	- **Technical:** Git-native. File-based. No external services required. Go for CLI and server.
325	- **Scope:** DDx manages documents, not agents. No workflow enforcement.
326	- **Platform:** macOS, Linux, Windows for CLI. Server runs anywhere Go runs.
327	- **License:** MIT, open source.
328	- **Agent safety:** DDx defaults to safe agent permissions. Permissive modes
329	  (`unrestricted`) require explicit opt-in via config or CLI flag. Normal
330	  users should never accidentally run agents with bypassed safety rails.
331	  See FEAT-006 Agent Permission Model.
332	
333	## Risks
334	
335	| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
336	|------|------------|--------|-----------|
337	| Documents go stale and get ignored | High | High | Reconciliation beads auto-generated on upstream changes; adversarial review agents check consistency; `ddx doctor` checks freshness |
338	| DDx/plugin boundary is fuzzy | Medium | High | Resolved for feedback (beads), metrics (FEAT-010 operational / FEAT-016 process), optimization (primitives in DDx, loop in plugins). Document remaining boundaries in FEAT-018. |
339	| Framework requires its author to explain it | High | High | Self-documenting project structures; getting-started guides bundled with plugins; team enablement as a P1 requirement |
340	| Agent testing and validation is unsolved industry-wide | Medium | High | DDx gives agents better context for first-pass correctness; adversarial review catches more issues; but fundamental problem remains open |
341	| MCP spec changes break server | Medium | Medium | Keep MCP integration thin; abstract behind internal API |
342	| Too much structure discourages adoption | Medium | Medium | Minimal defaults; let teams grow into structure |
343	| Rate of change in agentic ecosystem | High | Medium | Flexible plugin API; minimal DDx core; adapt without breaking plugin contracts |
344	| Git subtree complexity confuses users | Medium | Low | Wrap in simple commands; clear error messages |
345	
346	## Success Criteria
347	
348	- [ ] Users can set up DDx in a repository and manage project knowledge without
349	      relying on ad hoc file conventions
350	- [ ] Workflow tools can rely on DDx for shared work-item state instead of
351	      reimplementing local tracker storage
352	- [ ] Workflow tools can rely on DDx for agent dispatch and reusable invocation
353	      evidence
354	- [ ] Agents and tools can inspect repository documents and project state over
355	      local MCP or HTTP surfaces
356	- [ ] Website: live at ddx.github.io with clear messaging and embedded demos
357	- [ ] At least one workflow tool (HELIX) successfully building on DDx beads and
358	      agent dispatch
359	- [ ] `ddx install helix` bootstraps HELIX from the registry
360	- [ ] Document library syncing between 2+ projects
361	- [ ] CI pipeline green on every merge to main; Pages deploy gated on CI
362	- [ ] README includes animated terminal recordings of core workflows
363	- [ ] Upstream document changes auto-generate reconciliation beads for stale
364	      dependents
365	- [ ] Process metrics (bead cost, rework rate) queryable from existing data
366	- [ ] Multi-agent review workflow produces structured findings from quorum
367	      dispatch
368	- [ ] Plugin API is documented and stable enough for external plugin authors
369	- [ ] A new team member can orient in a DDx-initialized project without
370	      external explanation

5	# Product Vision: DDx
6	
7	**Version:** 2.1.0
8	**Date:** 2026-04-29
9	**Status:** Active
10	
11	## Core Thesis
12	
13	> Software is leverage. Fifty years of practice has shown that there are physics of software that must be respected to produce and maintain systems at scale. The agentic era allows every team to produce systems at scale and makes this even more important.
14	>
15	> Generative AI brings its own physics. DDx exists at the seam where these two meet — the fulcrum that lets software's lever do work. Without it, shipping software with agents quietly degrades into prompt-and-pray. The six principles below are the load-bearing claims on both sides.
16	
17	### Physics of Software
18	
19	1. **Abstraction is the lever.** Multi-level artifact stacks (vision → spec → test → code) with maintained relationships are how intent propagates without being lost. True for human teams; load-bearing for agents because they don't carry tacit knowledge between invocations.
20	2. **Software is iteration over tracked work.** Repeated trials over an explicit work substrate — beads, queues, dependency DAGs — is how software gets built. Pre-existed agents; agents make the substrate non-optional.
21	3. **Methodology is plural.** Different teams, projects, and problem domains demand different workflows — waterfall, agile, kanban, HELIX, ad-hoc. No tool that bakes one in survives the rest. DDx provides primitives (artifacts, runs, beads) that any methodology composes.
22	
23	### Physics of Generative AI
24	
25	4. **LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly.** Cost-tier ladders, ensemble verification, and 'cheapest model that works' are the operating shape of agentic work, not optimizations.
26	5. **Evidence provides memory.** Agents carry no state between invocations and outputs aren't bit-reproducible. The only thing that survives a run is what we captured as it happened. That captured evidence is the substrate for evaluation, trust, debugging, and learning — without it, every other principle degrades to anecdote.
27	
28	### The Intersection — DDx's Reason to Exist
29	
30	6. **Human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum.** Abstraction levers intent across the artifact stack, but only collaboration converts leverage into shipped software. Humans supply intent and accountability; AI supplies volume and execution. DDx is the toolkit at the seam — handoffs in both directions, at every level.
31	
32	## The Three-Layer Stack
33	
34	DDx is one layer in a three-project stack with explicit boundaries:
35	
36	| Layer | Project | Owns |
37	|-------|---------|------|
38	| Platform | **DDx** (this repo) | Document library, bead tracker, agent dispatch, personas, templates, git sync |
39	| Workflow | **HELIX** | Phases, gates, supervisory dispatch, bounded actions, methodology |
40	| Quality | **Dun** | Check discovery, execution, agent-friendly output |
41	
42	DDx provides primitives. HELIX and others provide opinions. Dun verifies the
43	result. Each layer is independently useful and replaceable.
44	
45	## Mission
46	
47	Make artifacts the unit of software development. Provide the shared
48	infrastructure that developers and workflow tools use to maintain, compose, and
49	deliver the artifacts agents produce and consume to build software.
50	
51	## What DDx Provides
52	
53	- **Artifact library** — structured, versioned, agent-discoverable artifacts in
54	  the repository. Documents, diagrams, wireframes, prompts, images, and other
55	  media; generators checked into git with source-hash-driven regeneration.
56	  Personas, patterns, templates, MCP server registry. Artifacts may be produced
57	  or consumed by humans and agents alike; DDx manages identity and provenance
58	  across all four combinations.
59	- **Multi-media artifact graph** — artifact identity, relationships, and content
60	  hashing across document and non-document media. Any file with a sidecar
61	  `.ddx.yaml` is a first-class artifact.
62	- **Bead tracker** — work items with a dependency DAG, ready/blocked queues,
63	  and JSONL interchange. Beads are the unit of work agents execute.
64	- **Three-layer run architecture** — `ddx run` (single agent invocation), `ddx
65	  try` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), `ddx work` (queue drain). Agent
66	  invocation is upstream; DDx owns worktree lifecycle, evidence bundling, and
67	  drain.
68	- **Project-local install** — `ddx init` and `ddx install <plugin>` only touch
69	  `<projectRoot>`. The only global artifact is `ddx-server`. No home-directory
70	  state, no machine-wide opinions.
71	- **Single `ddx` skill** — DDx ships one consolidated skill, not a fleet. One
72	  surface for agents to learn.
73	
74	## What DDx Is Not
75	
76	- **Not a methodology.** No phases, no gates, no prescribed artifact types.
77	  Workflow tools (HELIX) own those.
78	- **Not a storage system.** Files in Git. No proprietary backend.
79	- **Not an editor or IDE.** Editing happens wherever the user works.
80	- **Not opinionated about agents.** Any harness with a prompt-in/output-out
81	  contract plugs in.
82	
83	## Key Differentiators
84	
85	### vs. Ad-Hoc Agentic Coding
86	
87	Code-as-system-of-record produces point changes without coupling awareness, no
88	structured intent, and constant context re-explanation. DDx makes documents the
89	system of record for intent and architecture; code remains the system of record
90	for implementation.
91	
92	### vs. Code-Only Agent Tools
93	
94	Without an abstraction hierarchy, functionality accumulates in non-orthogonal
95	interfaces and agents cannot reason about cross-cutting impact. DDx gives every
96	change a defined place and a known impact boundary.
97	
98	### vs. Traditional Documentation-Driven Development
99	
100	Prior DDD failed because documents went stale. DDx counters that with
101	relationships, content hashing, beads as reconciliation tasks, and adversarial
102	review.
103	
104	## Design Philosophy
105	
106	### Platform vs Workflow Boundary
107	
108	The hardest discipline is keeping platform code free of methodology opinions.
109	The boundary is concrete:
110	
111	| DDx (Platform) | Workflow Tool (e.g., HELIX) |
112	|----------------|------------------------------|
113	| Document storage, versioning, sync | Artifact types and templates |
114	| Bead CRUD, dependency DAG, ready queue | Phase definitions, gates, ratchets |
115	| Agent dispatch, quorum, session logging | Methodology prompts, supervisory loops |
116	| Persona binding mechanism | Which personas fulfill which roles |
117	| Execute-loop runtime | When to run it, what to do with results |
118	
119	If a feature request would push opinions into the CLI, it belongs in a plugin.
120	
121	### Multi-Directional Iteration
122	
123	The artifact hierarchy is a set of lenses, not a pipeline. Vision changes
124	propagate down; implementation discoveries feed up; spec refinements ripple
125	both ways. DDx's primitives (beads, document relationships, content hashing)
126	support all three directions equally.
127	
128	### Cost-Tiered Execution
129	
130	Closed-bead throughput per dollar is the optimization target. Cheap models do
131	the work; stronger models review; deterministic checks (Dun) sit at the top of
132	the ladder catching what review missed. The agent service routes by capability,
133	not by name — endpoints with live model discovery, not hardcoded providers.
134	
135	### Three-Layer Run Architecture
136	
137	DDx owns three explicit layered primitives:
138	
139	| Layer | CLI | Owns |
140	|-------|-----|------|
141	| 1 | `ddx run` | Single agent invocation; structured output, tokens, model, exit metadata |
142	| 2 | `ddx try <bead>` | Bead attempt in isolated worktree; merge or preserve result |
143	| 3 | `ddx work` | Mechanical queue drain; no-progress detection and stop conditions |
144	
145	`ddx try` wraps `ddx run`. `ddx work` iterates `ddx try`. One on-disk
146	substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records. Agent invocation is upstream
147	(DDx-agent module via CONTRACT-003); DDx owns worktree lifecycle, evidence
148	bundling, and drain loop.
149	
150	### Human-Agent Control Slider
151	
152	DDx supports a continuum from full autonomy (drain the queue overnight) to
153	human-driven (agent assists, human commits). The same primitives serve both;
154	the workflow layer chooses where to sit.
155	
156	## Strategic Fit
157	
158	**Why us:** DDx grew out of daily agent-driven development. The problems we
159	solve are the problems we hit shipping software with agents.
160	
161	**Why now:** Document quality is the bottleneck. Every team is independently
162	rebuilding the same document infrastructure. No shared layer exists.
163	
164	**North Star:** Every developer working with AI agents uses DDx, or tools built
165	on DDx, to manage the documents that drive those agents.
166	
167	## Principles
168	
169	See `docs/helix/01-frame/principles.md`. The load-bearing ones:
170	
171	Operating principles are the choices DDx makes in response to the physics above.
172	
173	1. **Platform, not methodology** — DDx provides primitives; workflow tools provide opinions.
174	2. **Project-local by default** — install touches the project, not the machine.
175	3. **Artifacts are the product (documents primary, other media supported)** — code is output; artifacts are what you maintain.
176	4. **Bead-driven work** — beads are the unit of work; the queue is the interface.
177	5. **Cost-tiered throughput** — optimize closed beads per dollar, not raw capability.
178	6. **Git-native, file-first** — plain files, standard git, no lock-in.
179	7. **Agent-agnostic** — any capable harness plugs in via the prompt envelope.

AI-assisted development needs more than prompt files. Teams need a shared way
to manage declarative artifacts, reusable runtime evidence, and local
automation infrastructure without hardcoding workflow semantics into each tool.

The problems cluster by the physics they violate (see product-vision.md):

**Abstraction** (Principle 1 — abstraction is the lever)
- **No structure**: Artifacts, prompts, personas, and patterns accumulate as
  ad hoc files with weak identity and no explicit relationships
- **No composition**: Assembling the right combination of persona + pattern +
  spec into agent context is manual and error-prone
- **No document integrity guarantees**: When an upstream document changes,
  dependent documents silently drift — no automatic staleness detection or
  reconciliation tasking
- **No transferability**: Framework knowledge is trapped in its author;
  onboarding new team members requires manual explanation

**Iteration over tracked work** (Principle 2 — software is iteration over tracked work)
- **No reusable work-item store**: Workflow tools reimplement issue storage,
  dependency tracking, and coordination instead of sharing a local substrate
- **No reusable execution evidence**: Metrics, checks, and similar operations
  fall back to bespoke scripts and logs with no shared history model

**Methodology plurality** (Principle 3 — methodology is plural)
- **No reusable agent dispatch**: Each tool grows its own harness registry,
  logging, and output-capture behavior — every workflow tool reinvents the same
  invocation plumbing
- **No reuse**: Every project reinvents its agent instructions and supporting
  mechanics from scratch; proven patterns stay trapped in individual repos

**LLM physics** (Principle 4 — LLMs are stochastic, unreliable, and costly)
- **No cost-tier enforcement**: Token cost is a first-order constraint, not an
  optimization. Without capability-keyed routing and model-selection guidance,
  teams overspend on routine work and have no signal on the cheapest model that
  reliably closes beads

**Evidence and provenance** (Principle 5 — evidence provides memory)
- **No provenance for generated artifacts**: Generated files carry no record of
  which agent run, model, or prompt produced them — regeneration is ad hoc and
  lineage is lost
- **No measurement**: No standard way to track bead lifecycle metrics, token
  costs, or plugin-defined measures across projects
- **No feedback capture**: Lessons learned from agent interactions, project
  completions, and bead lifecycle stay informal — no structured way to capture,
  query, or act on what worked and what didn't

**Human-AI collaboration** (Principle 6 — human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum)
- **No composition for handoffs**: Assembling the right artifact context for a
  human-to-agent or agent-to-human handoff is manual and ad hoc — no DDx
  primitive covers that assembly
- **No discoverability**: Developers can't easily browse what documents,
  artifacts, or local runtime evidence are available
- **No network access**: Agents and tools can only read state from the local
  filesystem unless projects build their own HTTP or MCP layer

105	
106	AI-assisted development needs more than prompt files. Teams need a shared way

109	
110	The problems cluster by the physics they violate (see product-vision.md):
111

144	  which agent run, model, or prompt produced them — regeneration is ad hoc and
145	  lineage is lost

152	**Human-AI collaboration** (Principle 6 — human-AI collaboration is the fulcrum)

155	  primitive covers that assembly
156	- **No discoverability**: Developers can't easily browse what documents,
157	  artifacts, or local runtime evidence are available
158	- **No network access**: Agents and tools can only read state from the local
159	  filesystem unless projects build their own HTTP or MCP layer

161	## Goals
162	
163	### Primary
164	1. **Manage multi-media artifact libraries** — provide structure, conventions,
165	   and CLI tooling so declarative project knowledge — documents, diagrams,
166	   wireframes, images, prompts, and other media — stays organized and
167	   agent-discoverable
168	2. **Provide reusable local runtime services** — expose beads, agent dispatch,
169	   and execution history as workflow-agnostic DDx primitives
170	3. **Enable document composition** — combine personas, patterns, specs, and
171	   templates into coherent agent context
172	4. **Serve project state to agents and tools** — expose documents, artifacts,
173	   beads, and execution evidence via MCP endpoints and HTTP
174	5. **Support cross-project reuse** — share document libraries and workflow
175	   plugins through an online registry (`ddx install`)
176	6. **Provide agent-facing skills for DDx operations** — ship interactive
177	   skills (slash commands) that guide agents through complex DDx CLI
178	   operations like bead triage, agent dispatch, and package installation
179	7. **Integrate with revision control** — auto-commit document changes to
180	   protect work, expose document history to agents and tools, enable
181	   write-then-commit workflows for MCP and UI clients
182	8. **Support multi-agent coordination** — make bead operations, document
183	   writes, and agent dispatch safe under concurrent multi-agent use, with
184	   MCP as the remote observation and control surface
185	9. **Embed essential utilities** — bundle common developer tools (jq, etc.)
186	   so workflow tools have a consistent, cross-platform base without external
187	   runtime dependencies
188	10. **Maintain artifact graph integrity** — track relationships between
189	    documents, detect staleness when upstream artifacts change, and generate
190	    reconciliation tasks automatically (FEAT-007)
191	11. **Track process metrics** — derive bead lifecycle cost, rework rates, and
192	    other process measures from existing stores (beads, agent sessions) so
193	    teams can understand the economics and efficiency of their workflow
194	    (FEAT-016)
195	12. **Stabilize the plugin API** — document existing extension surfaces, add
196	    schema versioning, and commit to backward compatibility so plugin authors
197	    can build with confidence (FEAT-018)
198	13. **Provide a three-layer run architecture** — ship `ddx run` (single agent
199	    invocation), `ddx try` (bead attempt in isolated worktree), and `ddx work`
200	    (mechanical queue drain) as DDx-owned primitives on one unified on-disk
201	    substrate; layer metadata distinguishes records (FEAT-010)
202	14. **Enable source-hash-driven regeneration of generated artifacts** — track
203	    which agent run, model, and prompt produced each generated artifact;
204	    support on-demand regeneration when the source changes (FEAT-005, FEAT-007)
205	15. **Expose 100% of DDx state via HTTP and MCP read endpoints** — every
206	    piece of CLI-visible DDx state (artifacts, beads, run history, graph,
207	    sidecar metadata) is readable over the network; write surfaces are added
208	    case-by-case as workflows demand, starting with `artifactRegenerate`
209	    (FEAT-002, FEAT-010)
210	
211	### Secondary
212	1. **Promote the practice** — website explains document-driven development and
213	   drives adoption
214	2. **Keep artifacts honest** — detect drift between governing documents and
215	   lower-level artifacts or runtime evidence
216	3. **Enable team transferability** — self-documenting project structures,
217	   getting-started guides, and pairing workflows so DDx is productive without
218	   requiring its author in the room
219	
220	### Success Metrics
221	
222	| Metric | Target |
223	|--------|--------|
224	| Time to assemble agent context | <30 seconds |
225	| Document reuse rate across projects | >40% |
226	| MCP endpoint response time | <200ms |
227	| Website explains DDx clearly to new visitor | <2 minutes to understand |
228	
229	### Non-Goals
230	
231	- A workflow methodology (that's HELIX and others, not DDx)
232	- Workflow-specific artifact ladders or stage progression (for example,
233	  `FEAT -> SD -> TD -> TP`) beyond storing IDs and relationships
234	- Workflow-specific bead validation (phase labels, spec-id enforcement — that's
235	  the workflow layer via hooks)
236	- Supervisory loop orchestration — DDx owns mechanical queue drain (`ddx
237	  work`); content-aware supervisory decisions (deciding what to do next based
238	  on agent or execution results — for example, "comparison failed → enqueue
239	  reconciliation beads") remain plugin/HELIX territory
240	- Cataloging run types beyond the three layers — comparison, replay,
241	  benchmark, and similar workflow shapes are skill compositions; DDx does not
242	  enshrine them in Go core or specs
243	- An AI agent or agent framework
244	- A standalone desktop GUI for editing documents (the embedded web UI editor
245	  in `ddx-server` is in-scope per FEAT-008; a separate desktop application is not)
246	- A cloud/SaaS service
247	- Real-time collaboration
248	- A storage system — artifacts are versioned in Git; future backends are
249	  possible but not DDx's concern
250	- Defining artifact types or templates — those come from plugins. DDx provides
251	  the infrastructure for storing and relating them.
252	- Operational metric definitions — plugins define what to measure; DDx
253	  provides the execution and collection infrastructure (FEAT-010)
254	- Optimization loop logic — DDx provides primitives (run, measure, compare);
255	  plugins define what to try next and when to converge
256	- Feedback collection features — beads already capture structured feedback;
257	  no separate feedback subsystem needed
258	
259	## Users
260	
261	### Primary: Developer Using AI Agents
262	
263	**Role:** Professional developer directing AI agents and local automation
264	**Goals:** Keep project artifacts organized, compose context quickly, reuse
265	patterns across projects, inspect local execution evidence
266	**Pain:** Documents and evidence scattered everywhere, manual context assembly,
267	reinventing instructions and runtime tooling per project
268	
269	### Secondary: Workflow Tool Author
270	
271	**Role:** Developer building a methodology tool (like HELIX) on DDx primitives
272	**Goals:** Leverage DDx's document management, bead storage, agent dispatch,
273	execution history, persona system, and sync without reimplementing them
274	**Pain:** No standard infrastructure to build on; every workflow tool reinvents
275	local state, execution, and document management
276	
277	### Tertiary: Agent (Machine Consumer)
278	
279	**Role:** AI agent consuming documents via MCP or filesystem
280	**Goals:** Discover available documents and artifacts, read their contents,
281	understand their relationships, and inspect reusable runtime evidence
282	**Pain:** No programmatic way to browse document libraries or local execution

285	## Requirements

287	### Must Have (P0)

299	- dispatch supported AI agents through one reusable interface and inspect the
300	  resulting evidence
301	- validate installation and configuration health
302	- reuse and update shared DDx library content across projects
303	- invoke DDx operations through agent-facing skills (slash commands) that
304	  provide guided, validated workflows for complex CLI commands
305	- query process metrics (bead lifecycle cost, rework rates) derived from
306	  existing bead and agent session data
307	
308	**Plugin API**
309	The exact API contract lives in FEAT-018. At the PRD level, DDx must provide:
310	
311	- documentation of existing extension surfaces (package.yaml, plugin directory
312	  layout, SKILL.md format, hook scripts, bead conventions)
313	- schema versioning so plugin authors know what they can depend on
314	- backward compatibility commitment for documented surfaces
315	
316	**Server experience**
317	The exact server, HTTP, and MCP contracts live in FEAT-002. At the PRD level,
318	DDx must provide a local network surface that lets tools and agents:
319	
320	- browse and read document-library content remotely
321	- query artifact metadata, relationships, and staleness
322	- inspect shared work-item state
323	- inspect recorded agent session evidence
324	- rely on a stateless, filesystem-backed implementation rather than a hosted
325	  service
326	
327	**Website experience**
328	- Clear explanation of what DDx is and why it exists
329	- Quick start guide
330	- Link to CLI installation
331	- Link to documentation
332	- Embedded terminal recordings (asciinema) demonstrating core workflows
333	- README with animated demos that sell the tool at a glance
334	
335	**Release infrastructure**
336	- CI pipeline that runs the full test suite (via lefthook) on every push and PR
337	- E2E smoke tests validating the install-to-use journey
338	- Automated demo recording regeneration when CLI behavior changes
339	- GitHub Pages deployment gated on CI passing
340	- Multi-platform release builds with changelog generation
341	
342	### Should Have (P1)
343	
344	**CLI experience**
345	The CLI feature spec should also define requirements for:
346	
347	- assembling context from multiple DDx resources
348	- stronger document quality checks and health diagnostics
349	- generic execution definitions and immutable run history for evidence-producing
350	  operations
351	- higher-level projections over execution history for domains such as metrics
352	  and acceptance evidence
353	- process metrics derived from existing stores: bead lifecycle cost (time,
354	  tokens, rework), reopen rates, revision counts (FEAT-016)
355	
356	**Team enablement**
357	- getting-started guides that teach the platform alongside whichever plugin
358	  the user installs
359	- self-documenting project structures — after `ddx init` + plugin install,
360	  the project explains itself to new team members (human or agent)
361	- support for pairing workflows: structured onboarding where an experienced
362	  user guides a new team member through their first project
363	- internal project templates that teams can own end-to-end
364	
365	**Server experience**
366	The server feature spec should also define requirements for:
367	
368	- richer search across document-library contents
369	- persona resolution for remote consumers
370	- read-oriented access to generic execution definitions and run history
371	
372	**Website experience**
373	- Ecosystem page showing workflow tools built on DDx
374	- Document library browser (interactive)
375	- "See It In Action" section with recordings of end-to-end workflows
376	  (init, plugin install, project creation, feature evolution)
377	
378	### Nice to Have (P2)
379	
380	**CLI**
381	- Document testing (validate documents produce expected agent behavior)
382	- Document analytics (most reused, most effective)
383	- IDE integration for document browsing
384	
385	**Server**
386	- WebSocket notifications when documents change
387	- Multi-library aggregation (serve documents from multiple projects)
388	
389	## Constraints
390	
391	- **Technical:** Git-native. File-based. No external services required. Go for CLI and server.
392	- **Scope:** DDx manages documents, not agents. No workflow enforcement.
393	- **Platform:** macOS, Linux, Windows for CLI. Server runs anywhere Go runs.
394	- **License:** MIT, open source.
395	- **Agent safety:** DDx defaults to safe agent permissions. Permissive modes
396	  (`unrestricted`) require explicit opt-in via config or CLI flag. Normal
397	  users should never accidentally run agents with bypassed safety rails.
398	  See FEAT-006 Agent Permission Model.
399	
400	## Risks
401	
402	| Risk | Probability | Impact | Mitigation |
403	|------|------------|--------|-----------|
404	| Documents go stale and get ignored | High | High | Reconciliation beads auto-generated on upstream changes; adversarial review agents check consistency; `ddx doctor` checks freshness |
405	| DDx/plugin boundary is fuzzy | Medium | High | Resolved for feedback (beads), metrics (FEAT-010 operational / FEAT-016 process), optimization (primitives in DDx, loop in plugins). Document remaining boundaries in FEAT-018. |
406	| Framework requires its author to explain it | High | High | Self-documenting project structures; getting-started guides bundled with plugins; team enablement as a P1 requirement |
407	| Agent testing and validation is unsolved industry-wide | Medium | High | DDx gives agents better context for first-pass correctness; adversarial review catches more issues; but fundamental problem remains open |
408	| MCP spec changes break server | Medium | Medium | Keep MCP integration thin; abstract behind internal API |
409	| Too much structure discourages adoption | Medium | Medium | Minimal defaults; let teams grow into structure |
410	| Rate of change in agentic ecosystem | High | Medium | Flexible plugin API; minimal DDx core; adapt without breaking plugin contracts |
411	| Git subtree complexity confuses users | Medium | Low | Wrap in simple commands; clear error messages |
412	
413	## Success Criteria
414	
415	- [ ] Users can set up DDx in a repository and manage project knowledge without
416	      relying on ad hoc file conventions
417	- [ ] Workflow tools can rely on DDx for shared work-item state instead of
418	      reimplementing local tracker storage
419	- [ ] Workflow tools can rely on DDx for agent dispatch and reusable invocation
420	      evidence
421	- [ ] Agents and tools can inspect repository documents and project state over
422	      local MCP or HTTP surfaces
423	- [ ] Website: live at ddx.github.io with clear messaging and embedded demos
424	- [ ] At least one workflow tool (HELIX) successfully building on DDx beads and
425	      agent dispatch
426	- [ ] `ddx install helix` bootstraps HELIX from the registry
427	- [ ] Document library syncing between 2+ projects
428	- [ ] CI pipeline green on every merge to main; Pages deploy gated on CI
429	- [ ] README includes animated terminal recordings of core workflows
430	- [ ] Upstream document changes auto-generate reconciliation beads for stale
431	      dependents
432	- [ ] Process metrics (bead cost, rework rate) queryable from existing data
433	- [ ] Multi-agent review workflow produces structured findings from quorum
434	      dispatch
435	- [ ] Plugin API is documented and stable enough for external plugin authors
436	- [ ] A new team member can orient in a DDx-initialized project without
437	      external explanation
